hero.tagPill: BMW X6 • 2008–Present • 3 Generations (E71, F16, G06) • N54, N55, N63, S63, B58, B57, S68
hero.h1: X6 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every BMW X6 engine across three generations — from the N54 straight-six and N63 V8 of the E71 to the B58 straight-six, B57 diesel and S68 V8 of the G06. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for BMW's original Sports Activity Coupé. Backed by real UK X6 engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️ The X6 rule — the Sports Activity Coupé tax: an N63 recon at £6,000–£10,000+ against a £5,000–£8,000 E71 is 75–200% — ❌ scrap threshold. A B58 recon at £3,800–£6,000 against a £15,000–£25,000 G06 is 15–40% — ✅ safe. Generation and engine choice decide everything.
★★★★☆ — the B58 and B57 are the modern gold standard; the N55 is robust; the N63 V8 carries significant reliability caveats; the S63/S68 are proven M-car engines
B58B30 (G06 xDrive40i) — widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines
N63B44 (E71/F16 50i) — excessive oil consumption, valve stem seals, timing chain issues
2020+ G06 xDrive40i with B58 — the most dependable X6 powertrain ever made
G06 xDrive40d with B57 — 340 hp, 40+ mpg, bulletproof
N63 valve stem seals / oil consumption — shared-platform data [BMW-VERIFIED]
🏆 The Original Sports Activity Coupé — the car that created the coupé-SUV segment

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The X6 is BMW's original Sports Activity Coupé — three generations, 15 years, and one clear dividing line: the N63 V8 defines the high-risk era; the B58 and B57 define the modern gold standard. Buy the G06 with the B58 unless you have the budget for V8 bills — and on any E71 or F16 V8, budget for valve stem seals before you buy.

H2: BMW X6 Generations — Find Your Vehicle

rows.metric: Sub-headline: Three generations, 15 years, and the car that created the coupé-SUV segment. The right X6 depends on which generation and which engine you choose — and whether you're prepared for V8 bills.
BMW X6 E71 🔥 Most Enquired — classic era [BMW-VERIFIED]

rows.value: Our Verdict: The original X6 — BMW's first Sports Activity Coupé, based on the E70 X5 platform. The N54 and N55 straight-sixes are robust; the N63 V8 carries excessive oil consumption and valve stem seal issues. The M57 diesel is a bulletproof classic. Buy the six-cylinder or the M57 — the V8 is for the committed.

rows.metric: BMW X6 F16 🔥 Most Enquired — N63/N57 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]
rows.value: 2014–2019 • N55, N63, N57, B57 (late) — F15 X5 platform

Our Verdict: The second-generation X6 — evolutionary styling, refined interior, and available with the N63 V8 (improved but still carrying caveats) and N57 six-cylinder diesel. The N55 six-cylinder is robust; late models received the B57 diesel. Buy the six-cylinder or the diesel — the V8 is the trap.

rows.metric: BMW X6 G06 🔥 Most Enquired — B58/B57/S68 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]
rows.value: 2019–present • B58, B57, S68 (M60i), S63 (pre-LCI X6 M), S68 (LCI X6 M) — CLAR platform

Our Verdict: The third-generation X6 — CLAR platform, B-family engines, and the most dependable X6 ever made. The B58 in the xDrive40i is widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines. The B57 diesel delivers 340 hp and 40+ mpg. The S68 V8 in the M60i and X6 M Competition produces 530–625 hp with 48V mild-hybrid. This is the X6 generation to buy.
The Generation Divide — The Line That Matters Most
Read the full comparison: BMW X6 E71 vs F16 vs G06 →

rows.metric: The V8 Warning — N63 Valve Stem Seals and Oil Consumption
rows.value: Correction applied (internal): The N63 V8 (E71 50i, F16 50i) suffers from premature valve stem seal wear, causing blue smoke on startup and excessive oil consumption (1L per 1,000 miles). Timing chain stretch and fuel injector failure are also documented issues. BMW faced widespread criticism over these problems. The S63 (X6 M) and S68 (G06 M60i/X6 M Competition) are more robust but carry M-car servicing costs.

rows.metric: The Badge Ambiguity — "40i" and "50i" Mean Different Things
rows.value: Correction applied (internal): The "40i" badge covers different engines depending on generation. On the G06, "40i" means the B58B30 — the modern gold standard. On the E71, the "40i" didn't exist — it was badged as 35i/50i/M. The "50i" badge covers the N63 in the E71/F16 (high-risk) or the S68 in the G06 M60i (improved). All share a badge. None share a reliability profile.

engineDatabase.h2: BMW X6 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine ever fitted to a BMW X6 — verified codes, specifications and real UK ownership data.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: B58B30P, B57D30B, N63B44 and N55B30 ratings: 90% confidence — high-volume engines with deep specialist rebuild history and clear failure patterns. M57TUD30 and N57D30 ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — documented patterns from the E71/F16 era. E71 engines (N54, early N63): Pattern-data tier — documented by specialist consensus. S68B44A rating: Provisional — the G06 LCI M60i and X6 M Competition are too new for volume data; ratings reflect early patterns and shared-architecture evidence from the S63. Data note (internal) — corrections applied: Correction applied: The E71 X6 (2008–2014) used the N54 straight-six (2008–2010) and N55 straight-six (2010–2014) for petrol models. The N63 V8 was used for the xDrive50i. The S63 was used for the X6 M. Diesel models used the M57 and N57 engines. Correction applied: The N63 V8 (E71/F16 50i) carries excessive oil consumption, valve stem seal wear, timing chain stretch, and fuel injector failure issues. BMW recognized these problems and updated the engine in later years. Correction applied: The G06 X6 (2019–present) uses B-family engines for the main range — B58 (xDrive40i, 333–380 hp), B57 (xDrive40d, 340 hp) — and S63 (pre-LCI X6 M) or S68 (LCI M60i, X6 M Competition) for the M models. Correction applied: The X6 M (E71, F16, G06) uses the S63 engine (pre-2023) or S68 engine (2023+). The S68 adds 48V mild-hybrid assistance and produces 523–625 hp.

commonProblems.subHeadline: The six most common failure patterns across the X6 range — symptoms, costs and urgency.

problems.issue: N63 Valve Stem Seal & Oil Consumption
problems.description: The defining E71 and F16 X6 V8 issue — valve stem seals wear prematurely, causing blue smoke on startup and excessive oil consumption (1L per 1,000 miles). Affects E71/F16 50i (2008–2019). £6,000–£10,000+ recon.

problems.issue: N63 Timing Chain Stretch & Injector Failure
problems.description: The N63 V8 suffers from timing chain stretch and fuel injector failure. Rattling on cold start and rough running are warning signs. Affects E71/F16 50i. £6,000–£10,000+ recon.

The N63's hot-vee design causes cooling system failures (water pump, expansion tank) and turbocharger issues from heat soak. Affects E71/F16 50i. Cooling repairs: £500–£1,500; turbo repairs: £3,000–£6,000.
The B58's known weak point — coolant expansion tank leaks and water pump failures. Affects G06 xDrive40i (2019+). £400–£800 repair, not catastrophic if caught early.

The six-cylinder diesels' primary weak points — DPF clogging on urban-driven cars and injector issues. Affects E71 35d (M57), F16 30d/40d/M50d (N57). Well-documented, repairable, but the M57 is far more robust than the N57.
Not an engine failure — but a common non-engine X6 enquiry. The E71 and F16's xDrive transfer case can fail, causing driveline vibration and noise. Check on any high-mileage E71/F16 xDrive.

marketIntelligence.h2: BMW X6 Market Intelligence — What UK Owners Told Us in 2025

signals.demandTrend: ⬆ Rising — the most popular X6 generation [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]
⬇ Falling — the V8 issues drive buyers away [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]
⬆ Rising — the B58 is the modern gold standard

insights: Interesting insight: The X6 generates a different kind of enquiry than the X5 — buyers are often style-led, choosing the X6 for its coupé roofline over the X5's practicality. The engine questions are similar (N63 valve stem seals, B58 coolant), but the buyer profile is younger and more design-conscious. The X6 M generates a disproportionate share of enquiries — performance buyers doing due diligence on the S63/S68. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)

The E71 X6 is now entering bargain territory — £5,000–£8,000 for a used example. But the N63 V8 questions mean buyers need to budget accordingly: a £6,000–£10,000+ recon on a £5,000 car is a death sentence. The M57 3.0d E71 is the robust alternative — but they're rare and hold their value. The N55 six-cylinder E71s are also robust — but fuel economy is a consideration. (Editorial synthesis.)

The G06 X6 with the B58 or B57 is the generation to buy — the B-family engines are the most dependable X6 powertrains ever made. The CLAR platform is refined, the B58 is bulletproof, and the values are holding strong (£15,000–£25,000 for used examples). The X6 M Competition with the S68 is the ultimate X6 — 625 hp, 48V mild-hybrid, and 0-62 mph in 3.7 seconds. (Editorial synthesis.)

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW X6 Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: If you buy one X6 based on this page, buy a 2020+ G06 with the B58 straight-six — it's widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines, the CLAR platform is the most refined X6 ever, and the coupé styling is distinctive and desirable. If the budget only stretches to an E71 or F16, buy the six-cylinder (N54/N55) or the M57/N57 diesel — the V8s (N63) are the trap, not the bargain. And on any N63, budget for valve stem seals before you buy — it's £4,000–£8,000 well spent.

replacementCosts.h2: BMW X6 Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for used, reconditioned and rebuilt engines — supply only, with labour guidance.
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting a BMW X6 engine typically takes 12–24 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [BMW-QUOTE]. The X6 rule — the Sports Activity Coupé tax: An N63 recon at £6,000–£10,000+ against a £5,000–£8,000 E71 sits at 75–200% — ❌ walk away. A B58 recon at £3,800–£6,000 against a £15,000–£25,000 G06 is 15–40% — ✅ safe. Against a £70,000+ G06 X6 M Competition, an S68 recon at £9,000–£15,000+ is 13–21% — ✅ safe. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. The G06 with B58 or B57 is the one to buy; the E71/F16 N63 V8 is the one to avoid without valve stem seal history.

engineEvolution.h2: How the BMW X6 Engine Range Evolved — 2008 to Today

eras.whyBmwChanged: BMW launched the X6 as the first Sports Activity Coupé — the N54/N55 straight-sixes and M57 diesel were robust; the N63 V8 carried significant caveats.
eras.worthKnowing: The N63 V8's valve stem seal and oil consumption issues define this era. The M57 35d is the bulletproof classic. Buy the six-cylinder or the M57 — the V8 is for the committed.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The second-generation X6 refined the package — the N55 six-cylinder is robust; the updated N63 improved but still carries caveats. The B57 diesel arrived late.
eras.worthKnowing: The F16 is the X6 for buyers who want a modern SUV without the G06 price tag — buy the N55 or N57.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The third-generation X6 arrived on the CLAR platform — B-family engines for the main range, S63/S68 for the M models. The B58 is one of BMW's most reliable modern engines. The S68 V8 arrived in 2023 with 48V mild-hybrid.
eras.worthKnowing: The G06 is the X6 to buy — B-family engines are the most dependable X6 powertrains ever made. The B58 xDrive40i is the sweet spot; the X6 M Competition with the S68 is the ultimate X6.

whoShouldBuy.h2: Who Should Buy a BMW X6?

profiles.verdict: The X6 is built for this — the coupé roofline and distinctive design make it the style-led alternative to the X5. The G06 with B58 is the pick; the M60i adds performance without sacrificing style.
The X6 is a great commuter — the G06 xDrive40d with B57 returns 40+ mpg, the CLAR platform is comfortable, and the coupé styling makes it stand out. The G06 is even more refined.

A cheap E71 N63 is not a cheap X6 — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody. A £15,000 G06 with B58 is the bargain; a £5,000 E71 50i without valve stem seal history is the trap. The E71/F16 six-cylinder or diesel are the safer budget options.

The G06 X6 M Competition with the S68 is a genuine performance SUV — 625 hp, xDrive, and 0-62 mph in 3.7 seconds. The M60i is the warm-up act — 523 hp and 80% of the M's performance for less money. Both are the ultimate X6s.

The E71 35d with the M57 is a classic keeper — one of BMW's most durable diesels. The G06 xDrive40i with the B58 is the modern keeper — the B58 will outlast the car around it. Both are worth proper investment.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My BMW X6 Engine — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: Blue smoke on startup from an N63? Coolant loss on a B58? On the X6, the answer depends on which generation and which engine you have — an E71 N63 and a G06 B58 face very different economics. Get a personalised repair-vs-replace verdict using real UK cost data.

items.question: How much does a BMW X6 engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned BMW X6 engine typically costs £3,800–£10,000+ supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — from the B58 at £3,800–£6,000 to the N63 at £6,000–£10,000+. The S63/S68 costs £9,000–£15,000+. Fitting adds £650–£2,100. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

items.question: Which BMW X6 engine is the most reliable?
items.answer: The B58B30 straight-six in the G06 xDrive40i — widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines. The B57D30 diesel is also excellent. The M57TUD30 in the E71 is the classic robust diesel pick.

items.question: Which BMW X6 engine should I avoid?
items.answer: No engine outright — but an E71 or F16 N63B44 without documented valve stem seal replacement, timing chain history, or oil consumption history is the one purchase we consistently advise against. A G06 B58 or B57 has no such caveat.

items.question: How do I know if my X6 engine needs replacing?
items.answer: By engine: blue smoke on startup (N63 valve stem seals), rattling on cold start (N63 timing chain), coolant loss (B58 cooling system), DPF clogging (M57/N57/B57). On the E71/F16 V8s, blue smoke on startup is a warning sign. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £3,800–£10,000+ engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

items.question: Is it worth replacing a BMW X6 engine?
items.answer: Depends entirely on generation and engine. Against a £15,000–£25,000 G06, a £3,800–£6,000 B58 recon is 15–40% — ✅ safe. Against a £5,000–£8,000 E71, a £6,000–£10,000+ N63 recon is 75–200% — ❌ walk away. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.

items.question: Which BMW X6 should I buy?
items.answer: 2020+ G06 with the B58 xDrive40i — the most dependable X6 ever made, 333–380 hp, the B58 is bulletproof, and the values are holding strong (£15,000–£25,000). For diesel, G06 xDrive40d with B57 — 340 hp, 40+ mpg. For the budget option, E71 with M57 35d or N55 35i — the robust classics. Avoid E71 and F16 V8s without documented valve stem seal history.

items.question: How do I find my BMW X6 engine code?
items.answer: Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation and variant (E71 35d, E71 50i, F16 50i, G06 40i) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

items.question: BMW X6 vs X5 — which is more reliable?
items.answer: Mechanically they share engines across similar eras — the N54/N55/N63/B58/B57 story is identical. The X6 is essentially the X5 with a coupé roofline; reliability is the same. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The X5 offers more practicality; the X6 offers more style. Read the full head-to-head →
